American producer, songwriter, and musician

Musical artist

**Michael A. Elizondo Jr.** (born October 22, 1972) is an American [producer](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer"), [songwriter](/wiki/Songwriter "Songwriter"), and [multi-instrumentalist](/wiki/Multi-instrumentalist "Multi-instrumentalist"). A protégé of [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre"), Elizondo has worked with [50 Cent](/wiki/50_Cent "50 Cent"), [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park"), [Carrie Underwood](/wiki/Carrie_Underwood "Carrie Underwood"), [Fiona Apple](/wiki/Fiona_Apple "Fiona Apple"), [Mastodon](/wiki/Mastodon_(band) "Mastodon (band)"), [Ry Cooder](/wiki/Ry_Cooder "Ry Cooder"), [Twenty One Pilots](/wiki/Twenty_One_Pilots "Twenty One Pilots"), [Nelly Furtado](/wiki/Nelly_Furtado "Nelly Furtado"), [Brothers Osborne](/wiki/Brothers_Osborne "Brothers Osborne"), [Lauren Daigle](/wiki/Lauren_Daigle "Lauren Daigle"), [Madison Cunningham](/wiki/Madison_Cunningham "Madison Cunningham"), and [Lin-Manuel Miranda](/wiki/Lin-Manuel_Miranda "Lin-Manuel Miranda"), among others. His songwriting credits include "[In da Club](/wiki/In_da_Club "In da Club")" by 50 Cent, Eminem's "[Just Lose It](/wiki/Just_Lose_It "Just Lose It")" and "[The Real Slim Shady](/wiki/The_Real_Slim_Shady "The Real Slim Shady")", "[Family Affair](/wiki/Family_Affair_(Mary_J._Blige_song) "Family Affair (Mary J. Blige song)")" by [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige"), and Carrie Underwood's "[Cowboy Casanova](/wiki/Cowboy_Casanova "Cowboy Casanova")".[[1]](#cite_note-song_u-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-star_trib-3) He has won three [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") from seven nominations, which includes two nominations for [Producer of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Producer_of_the_Year,_Non-Classical "Grammy Award for Producer of the Year, Non-Classical").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Elizondo was born and raised in Pacoima, California. His musician father, Miguel, had a diverse record collection and Elizondo grew up listening to artists from the Beatles to Motown to Johnny Cash. His father hosted jam sessions at home, and in the early 80s converted the garage into a recording studio to record local bands.

Elizondo learned to play the accordion at age 9 and the tenor saxophone at 12. At 14, he picked up the electric bass, which became his main instrument, and played in a metal band. In a 2006 interview with *[Sound on Sound](/wiki/Sound_on_Sound "Sound on Sound"),* he said: "The reason that playing bass stuck was that the role it has, whether you're playing acoustic or electric or keyboard bass, is very appealing to me, because you are the link between the rhythm and the melody."[[6]](#cite_note-soundonsound-6)

He went to [Hamilton High School Music Academy](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_High_School_(Los_Angeles) "Alexander Hamilton High School (Los Angeles)"), where he met future engineer [Manny Marroquin](/wiki/Manny_Marroquin "Manny Marroquin") and drummer [Abe Laboriel Jr.](/wiki/Abe_Laboriel_Jr. "Abe Laboriel Jr."), who inspired him to devote more time to practicing in order to improve his technique. He discovered an affinity for jazz, and while he continued to play the electric bass with hardcore and metal bands he formed, in his senior year he picked up the [upright bass](/wiki/Jazz_bass "Jazz bass"), studying classically to understand the full scope of the instrument.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Elizondo met bassist and [California State University, Northridge](/wiki/California_State_University,_Northridge "California State University, Northridge") (CSUN) music professor Gary Pratt when Pratt guest-conducted Hamilton's jazz band. Elizondo subsequently studied privately with Pratt, who encouraged Elizondo to apply to CSUN. He was accepted, and enrolled in the university's music program in 1991.[[9]](#cite_note-CSUN-9)

Already well-versed in jazz and other genres of music, Elizondo's emphasis was on classical music at CSUN; as he studied with Pratt, in addition to Ed Meares,[[10]](#cite_note-10) he played in chamber groups and the school orchestra. He also played in bands with musicians such as [Nels and](/wiki/Nels_Cline "Nels Cline") [Alex Cline](/wiki/Alex_Cline "Alex Cline"), [Vinny Golia](/wiki/Vinny_Golia "Vinny Golia"), [Peter Erskine](/wiki/Peter_Erskine "Peter Erskine"), and in 1994, as his extracurricular gigs became more time-consuming, he left CSUN to focus professionally on music full-time.[[9]](#cite_note-CSUN-9)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1990s")]

#### Budahat, session work, The Firm, Poe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Budahat, session work, The Firm, Poe")]

At [CSUN](/wiki/CSUN "CSUN"), Elizondo played bass and wrote songs for Budahat, a Rock/R&B band that he founded with a childhood friend, Trevor Lawrence, Jr.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1995, the band was signed by Atlantic. Although their album was never released,[[13]](#cite_note-Hits-13) the experience provided Elizondo with an education in publishing and the record industry, and introduced him as a songwriter and musician to prominent producers including [T-Bone Burnett](/wiki/T-Bone_Burnett "T-Bone Burnett"), [Glen Ballard](/wiki/Glen_Ballard "Glen Ballard"), [Matthew Wilder](/wiki/Matthew_Wilder "Matthew Wilder"), and [Matt Wallace](/wiki/Matt_Wallace_(record_producer) "Matt Wallace (record producer)"). As he became more in-demand as a session musician, he played on records by artists including [Sheryl Crow](/wiki/Sheryl_Crow "Sheryl Crow"), [Ry Cooder](/wiki/Ry_Cooder "Ry Cooder"), [Fiona Apple](/wiki/Fiona_Apple "Fiona Apple"), [Perla Batalla](/wiki/Perla_Batalla "Perla Batalla").[[1]](#cite_note-song_u-1)[[14]](#cite_note-credits-14)

In 1996, Elizondo was booked as a session musician for the recording of *[The Album](/wiki/The_Album_(The_Firm_album) "The Album (The Firm album)")*, by [The Firm](/wiki/The_Firm_(hip_hop_group) "The Firm (hip hop group)"), a hip hop supergroup composed of [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), [Foxy Brown](/wiki/Foxy_Brown_(rapper) "Foxy Brown (rapper)"), [AZ](/wiki/AZ_(rapper) "AZ (rapper)"), and [Nature](/wiki/Nature_(rapper) "Nature (rapper)"). Produced by Dr. Dre and the [Trackmasters](/wiki/Trackmasters "Trackmasters"), it was one of the first records released on Dre's then newly founded [Aftermath](/wiki/Aftermath_Entertainment "Aftermath Entertainment") label. The Firm sessions marked the start of Elizondo's relationship with Dre.[[6]](#cite_note-soundonsound-6)

Elizondo met [Poe](/wiki/Poe_(singer) "Poe (singer)") through Matt Wallace in 1997, and co-wrote four songs for her second Atlantic album, *[Haunted](/wiki/Haunted_(Poe_album) "Haunted (Poe album)")*, including its title track. Following the release of the album, he was signed to a publishing deal with producer [Steve Lindsey](/wiki/Steve_Lindsey "Steve Lindsey"), who had a co-venture with Windswept Pacific. Elizondo later said: "Steve was the one early on, who truly believed in my potential as a songwriter."[[1]](#cite_note-song_u-1)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000s")]

#### Dr. Dre, Eminem, *Get Rich or Die Tryin'*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Dr. Dre, Eminem, Get Rich or Die Tryin'")]

Shortly after he signed his publishing deal, Elizondo began to work steadily on Aftermath projects as part of Dre's core creative team.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Borgmeyer,_John._2007_119-16) He played on Dre's solo album [2001](/wiki/2001_(Dr._Dre_album) "2001 (Dr. Dre album)") in 1999, and continued to work closely with him for the next 11 years as a multi-instrumentalist (bass, keyboards, and guitars), songwriter, and producer.[[17]](#cite_note-Bass_Player-17)

Elizondo—who was with Dre when he and Eminem met[[16]](#cite_note-Borgmeyer,_John._2007_119-16)—scored his first co-writing credit with Dre for "[The Real Slim Shady](/wiki/The_Real_Slim_Shady "The Real Slim Shady")" from Eminem's *[Marshall Mathers LP](/wiki/The_Marshall_Mathers_LP "The Marshall Mathers LP")*. Written with Tommy Coster, Jr., the song "made Eminem a phenomenon",[[18]](#cite_note-18) with *The Marshall Mathers LP* selling more than 1.8 million albums in its first week. "The Real Slim Shady" won a Grammy, hit the US Top 10, and entered the UK chart at No. 1. As of 2019, with more than 35 million in worldwide sales, the record was the top-selling rap album of all time.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Elizondo's first co-production credits were for "True Lies" and "Lay Low" on [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg")'s 2000 album *[The Last Meal](/wiki/The_Last_Meal "The Last Meal")*. [Scott Storch](/wiki/Scott_Storch "Scott Storch"), another of Dre's proteges, also co-produced "Lay Low".[[20]](#cite_note-hotnewhiphop.com-20) In
2003, in addition to playing bass, guitar and keyboards, Elizondo co-wrote five songs on 50 Cent's *[Get Rich or Die Trying](/wiki/Get_Rich_or_Die_Trying "Get Rich or Die Trying")*, and co-wrote and co-produced the album's first and final singles, "[In da Club](/wiki/In_da_Club "In da Club")" and "[If I Can't](/wiki/If_I_Can%27t "If I Can't")". A "minimalist masterpiece,"[[21]](#cite_note-21) "In da Club" was No. 1 for nine of the 30 weeks it charted on the *Billboard* Hot 100, and broke the *Billboard* record as the most listened to song in radio history within a week of its release. It was No. 24 on the decade-long Hot 100, and #13 on the *Rolling Stone* list of the best songs of the decade.[[22]](#cite_note-22) His run of hits continued through the end of 2004, with [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige")'s "[Family Affair](/wiki/Family_Affair_(Mary_J._Blige_song) "Family Affair (Mary J. Blige song)")" charting at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100; "[Let Me Blow Ya Mind](/wiki/Let_Me_Blow_Ya_Mind "Let Me Blow Ya Mind")" by Eve ft. Gwen Stefani, and "[Rich Girl](/wiki/Rich_Girl_(Gwen_Stefani_song) "Rich Girl (Gwen Stefani song)")" by Gwen Stefani featuring [Eve](/wiki/Eve_(rapper) "Eve (rapper)"), which hit the Top 10. He co-wrote four songs on *[The Eminem Show](/wiki/The_Eminem_Show "The Eminem Show")* and eight songs on Eminem's *[Encore](/wiki/Encore_(Eminem_album) "Encore (Eminem album)")*, including "[Just Lose It](/wiki/Just_Lose_It "Just Lose It")", an international hit.[[20]](#cite_note-hotnewhiphop.com-20)[[14]](#cite_note-credits-14)

#### Fiona Apple, Producer of the Year, Carrie Underwood, Maroon 5

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fiona Apple, Producer of the Year, Carrie Underwood, Maroon 5")]

As Elizondo's success in the hip hop and rap genres continued to grow, he was sought out to work with pop, rock, roots and alternative artists.[[14]](#cite_note-credits-14) In 2005, he was recruited to produce Fiona Apple's 2005 album, *[Extraordinary Machine](/wiki/Extraordinary_Machine "Extraordinary Machine")*.[[23]](#cite_note-23) *Extraordinary Machine* received almost universal acclaim, with *Rolling Stone* writing that Elizondo had given the tracks energy, taking a "sad song and making it better."[[24]](#cite_note-24) That same year, he worked with [Mavis Staples](/wiki/Mavis_Staples "Mavis Staples"), Ry Cooder, [Sheryl Crow](/wiki/Sheryl_Crow "Sheryl Crow"), [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper"), [Burt Bacharach](/wiki/Burt_Bacharach "Burt Bacharach") and [Avril Lavigne](/wiki/Avril_Lavigne "Avril Lavigne"), and in 2006, he played, wrote and/or produced music by Mary J. Blige, [Gwen Stefani](/wiki/Gwen_Stefani "Gwen Stefani"), [T-Bone Burnett](/wiki/T-Bone_Burnett "T-Bone Burnett"), [Doyle Bramhall II](/wiki/Doyle_Bramhall_II "Doyle Bramhall II"), Christina Aguilera, and [P!nk](/wiki/Pink_(singer) "Pink (singer)").[[25]](#cite_note-25) He also produced the [Alanis Morissette](/wiki/Alanis_Morissette "Alanis Morissette") song "[Wunderkind](/wiki/Wunderkind "Wunderkind")", which appeared on [The Lion, the Witch, and the Wardrobe](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia:_The_Lion,_the_Witch_and_the_Wardrobe_(soundtrack) "The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe (soundtrack)") soundtrack and was nominated in the Best Original Song category at the 2006 [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Seeking to "toughen up their sound," [Maroon 5](/wiki/Maroon_5 "Maroon 5") hired Elizondo to produce their second album, *[It Won't Be Soon Before Long](/wiki/It_Won%27t_Be_Soon_Before_Long "It Won't Be Soon Before Long")* in 2007. 17 tracks were recorded over a period of 8 months, and 14 made the record, including "[If I Never See Your Face Again](/wiki/If_I_Never_See_Your_Face_Again "If I Never See Your Face Again")", featuring [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna"), which would also appear on her album, *[Good Girl Gone Bad: Reloaded](/wiki/Good_Girl_Gone_Bad:_Reloaded "Good Girl Gone Bad: Reloaded")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The album entered the charts at No. 1 in the UK and the US, where it sold nearly 403,000 copies in its first week.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The reviews were positive, with *AllMusic* critic [Stephen Thomas Erlewine](/wiki/Stephen_Thomas_Erlewine "Stephen Thomas Erlewine") writing: "*It Won't Be Soon Before Long* is that rare self-stylized blockbuster album that sounds as big and satisfying as was intended." The single "[Makes Me Wonder](/wiki/Makes_Me_Wonder "Makes Me Wonder")" was nominated for a Grammy, and the album was the top selling digital LP of 2007.[[29]](#cite_note-AMG-29)

Similarly, Elizondo was brought in to help Rilo Kiley make their fourth album, *[Under the Blacklight](/wiki/Under_the_Blacklight "Under the Blacklight")*, more rhythmic. Drummer Jason Boesel said, in discussing the title track, "Elizondo came in and answered our question of how we could get it to feel dancier. He stripped it down, and made it kind of a banger." Acknowledged as a collaborative effort between Elizondo and the band, the album earned "rave reviews"[[30]](#cite_note-30) with the BBC stating that it went "one step further in its bittersweetly effortless pop."[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Elizondo was nominated for the Grammy Award for Producer of the Year in 2008 based on his body of work between October 1, 2007, and September 20, 2008.[[14]](#cite_note-credits-14) The nomination brought increased attention to his multi-genre credits, resulting in a "slew of new projects that propelled him beyond the pop and hip-hop genres in which he had made his name."[[17]](#cite_note-Bass_Player-17)

Over the following two years, working frequently out of Phantom Studios, a 1000-foot recording studio Elizondo built at his home, he produced four tracks on [Regina Spektor](/wiki/Regina_Spektor "Regina Spektor")'s *[Far](/wiki/Far_(album) "Far (album)")*; wrote with [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake") ("[Love Sex Magic](/wiki/Love_Sex_Magic "Love Sex Magic")"), and produced *[Holy Smoke](/wiki/Holy_Smoke_(Gin_Wigmore_album) "Holy Smoke (Gin Wigmore album)")*, the debut album by [Gin Wigmore](/wiki/Gin_Wigmore "Gin Wigmore"). He also worked with [Jay Z](/wiki/Jay_Z "Jay Z") and [Leona Lewis](/wiki/Leona_Lewis "Leona Lewis").[[32]](#cite_note-32) In 2009 he went to Nashville to write with Carrie Underwood, and during their second session, together with Brett James, they wrote "[Cowboy Casanova](/wiki/Cowboy_Casanova "Cowboy Casanova")." The first single from her third studio album, it was a number one hit, and went on to sell more than 2.3 million records.[[33]](#cite_note-33) That same year, he wrote five songs for Eminem's *[Relapse](/wiki/Relapse_(Eminem_album) "Relapse (Eminem album)")*—an album he also played on—and produced Switchfoot's *[Hello Hurricane](/wiki/Hello_Hurricane "Hello Hurricane"),* the album's "varied arrangements reflecting his diverse résumé."[[34]](#cite_note-34) Elizondo won a Grammy Award in the Best Rock Gospel album category for his work on *Hello Hurricane.*

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2010s")]

#### Avenged Sevenfold, Warner Bros., Keith Urban

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Avenged Sevenfold, Warner Bros., Keith Urban")]

[Avenged Sevenfold](/wiki/Avenged_Sevenfold "Avenged Sevenfold") brought Elizondo in to produce their fifth album, *[Nightmare](/wiki/Nightmare_(Avenged_Sevenfold_album) "Nightmare (Avenged Sevenfold album)")*, in 2010. On choosing to work with Elizondo, guitarist Zacky Vengeance said: "Mike Elizondo loves Metallica, Megadeth and Iron Maiden, but he's also a musician and artist, and he's never worked on a rock album. So it seemed perfect, because we wanted to come up with something new and creative."[[35]](#cite_note-35) *Nightmare* debuted at No. 1 on the *Billboard* album charts.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In January 2011, recruited by [Lenny Waronker](/wiki/Lenny_Waronker "Lenny Waronker") and [Rob Cavallo](/wiki/Rob_Cavallo "Rob Cavallo"),[[13]](#cite_note-Hits-13) Elizondo was named Sr. VP of A&R and Staff Producer at Warner Bros. Records.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Mainly a creative role, he focused jointly on A&R and production, working primarily with Warner Bros. artists, including [Gary Clark Jr](/wiki/Gary_Clark_Jr "Gary Clark Jr"), [Mastodon](/wiki/Mastodon_(band) "Mastodon (band)"), [Kimbra](/wiki/Kimbra "Kimbra"), [Jenny Lewis](/wiki/Jenny_Lewis "Jenny Lewis"), [The Regrettes](/wiki/The_Regrettes "The Regrettes"), [JR JR](/wiki/JR_JR "JR JR"), [Eli "Paperboy" Reed](/wiki/Eli_%22Paperboy%22_Reed "Eli \"Paperboy\" Reed"), [Daye Jack](/wiki/Daye_Jack "Daye Jack"), and [Echosmith](/wiki/Echosmith "Echosmith"), the first band he signed.[[13]](#cite_note-Hits-13) In addition, he produced Avenged Sevenfold's follow-up to *[Nightmare](/wiki/Nightmare_(Avenged_Sevenfold_album) "Nightmare (Avenged Sevenfold album)")*, *[Hail to the King](/wiki/Hail_to_the_King_(Avenged_Sevenfold_album) "Hail to the King (Avenged Sevenfold album)")*, and played on and co-produced [Gary Clark, Jr.](/wiki/Gary_Clark,_Jr. "Gary Clark, Jr.")'s *[Blak and Blu](/wiki/Blak_and_Blu "Blak and Blu")*,[[38]](#cite_note-38) and in 2012 reunited with Regina Spektor to produce her album, *[What We Saw from the Cheap Seats](/wiki/What_We_Saw_from_the_Cheap_Seats "What We Saw from the Cheap Seats").*[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 2013, to "keep things fresh for himself and his fans," [Keith Urban](/wiki/Keith_Urban "Keith Urban") collaborated with Elizondo, who co-wrote and co-produced two tracks on his album, *[Fuse](/wiki/Fuse_(Keith_Urban_album) "Fuse (Keith Urban album)").* A fan of Elizondo's work with Dre, in an interview with the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* Urban said: "He has a great rhythmic gift as a bass player, is an incredible drum programmer and multi-instrumentalist. My music is very, very rhythmic-based...I wanted to explore a much stronger presence of rhythmic elements in my music. It's something that's been there, but often has been pushed to the back." The album debuted at No. 1 in Australia and the US.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

#### Twenty One Pilots, Hamilton Mixtape, *Live from Here*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Twenty One Pilots, Hamilton Mixtape, Live from Here")]

In 2015, Elizondo was contacted by [Lin-Manuel Miranda](/wiki/Lin-Manuel_Miranda "Lin-Manuel Miranda") to develop *[The Hamilton Mixtape](/wiki/The_Hamilton_Mixtape "The Hamilton Mixtape"),* which would be based on (but separate from) *[Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton_(play) "Hamilton (play)")*. Using live cast recordings and a verse from [Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots") frontman Black Thought, Elizondo created a version of the musical's "[My Shot](/wiki/My_Shot "My Shot")" that became the first song on *The Hamilton Mixtape*, "setting the standard and attitude of the project." Elizondo also produced "Satisfied," by [Sia](/wiki/Sia "Sia") (featuring [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_(singer) "Miguel (singer)") and [Queen Latifah](/wiki/Queen_Latifah "Queen Latifah")) and [Andra Day](/wiki/Andra_Day "Andra Day")'s cover of "[Burn](/wiki/Burn_(Hamilton_song) "Burn (Hamilton song)")", giving it a "classic, old-school R&B vibe with hip-hop drums and overtones."[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Elizondo also produced four songs on [Twenty One Pilots](/wiki/Twenty_One_Pilots "Twenty One Pilots") breakout album *[Blurryface](/wiki/Blurryface "Blurryface")* in 2015. The second single from the album, "[Stressed Out](/wiki/Stressed_Out "Stressed Out")" reached number two on the *Billboard* Hot 100, and was the first rock song to surpass 1 billion streams on Spotify. In 2016, with 1.5 billion views on YouTube, "Stressed Out" won the Grammy Award for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance. *Blurryface* hit No. 1 on the album charts, and remained in the Top 200 for more than four years straight. It was the first album in the digital era to have every track certified gold by the [RIAA](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In September 2018, Elizondo became the music director of *Live from Here with Chris Thile* (formerly *A Prairie Home Companion with Chris Thile*). Elizondo played bass on several early episodes of the show and was offered the full-time music director gig in July 2018. In deciding to accept the offer—a 20-hour a week commitment, which required him to travel—he said: "On a creative level, I was extremely honored, but on a personal level, I had to figure out how to orchestrate my life. It's a lot more traveling than I'm used to. I finally realized that the energy I'm going to gain doing these shows will help fuel my enthusiasm for all the other projects I'm working on."[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Among others, during the last half of the decade, Elizondo worked with [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran"), the [Jonas Bros.](/wiki/Jonas_Bros. "Jonas Bros."), [Young the Giant](/wiki/Young_the_Giant "Young the Giant"), [K.Flay](/wiki/K.Flay "K.Flay"), [NF](/wiki/NF_(rapper) "NF (rapper)"), [Walk the Moon](/wiki/Walk_the_Moon "Walk the Moon") and [Brantley Gilbert](/wiki/Brantley_Gilbert "Brantley Gilbert"). He also produced Gary Clark Jr.'s cover of "[Come Together](/wiki/Come_Together "Come Together")" with [Junkie XL](/wiki/Junkie_XL "Junkie XL") for the [*Justice League* soundtrack](/wiki/Justice_League_(soundtrack) "Justice League (soundtrack)").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020s")]

#### Producer of the Year 2022 Grammy nomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Producer of the Year 2022 Grammy nomination")]

In 2021, Elizondo was nominated for the 2022 Producer of the Year Grammy Award, non-classical. The nomination is based on his body of work during the eligibility period, which included co-production on the *[Encanto](/wiki/Encanto_(soundtrack) "Encanto (soundtrack)")* soundtrack.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Teamed with co-producer Lin-Manuel Miranda, the film's "[We Don't Talk About Bruno](/wiki/We_Don%27t_Talk_About_Bruno "We Don't Talk About Bruno")" was #1 on the *Billboard* charts for 5 weeks, becoming the longest-running #1 song in Disney's history.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) In 2023, *Encanto* won the Grammy for Best Compilation Soundtrack for Visual Media.[[50]](#cite_note-:0-50)

Other notable projects during this time include [Turnstile](/wiki/Turnstile_(band) "Turnstile (band)")'s album *[Glow On](/wiki/Glow_On "Glow On")*, which earned three Grammy nominations, [Madison Cunningham's](/wiki/Madison_Cunningham "Madison Cunningham") *[Revealer](/wiki/Revealer_(album) "Revealer (album)")*, which won Folk Album of the Year in 2023,[[50]](#cite_note-:0-50) and work with artists such as [Brothers Osborne](/wiki/Brothers_Osborne "Brothers Osborne"), [Lauren Daigle](/wiki/Lauren_Daigle "Lauren Daigle"), [Lake Street Dive](/wiki/Lake_Street_Dive "Lake Street Dive"), [Sheryl Crow](/wiki/Sheryl_Crow "Sheryl Crow"), and [Alessia Cara](/wiki/Alessia_Cara "Alessia Cara"). He has also produced the [Lin-Manuel Miranda](/wiki/Lin-Manuel_Miranda "Lin-Manuel Miranda") and [Eisa Davis](/wiki/Eisa_Davis "Eisa Davis") [concept album](/wiki/Concept_album "Concept album") *[Warriors](/wiki/Warriors_(Lin-Manuel_Miranda_and_Eisa_Davis_album) "Warriors (Lin-Manuel Miranda and Eisa Davis album)")*.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mike_Elizondo&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Elizondo and his wife, Trista, moved from Los Angeles to Nashville in 2018. Married in 1997, they have four children.[[52]](#cite_note-52) His recording studio is in [Gallatin, Tennessee](/wiki/Gallatin,_Tennessee "Gallatin, Tennessee").[[53]](#cite_note-53)
